Assertion (A): The number 5ⁿ never ends with digit 0 for any natural number n.
Reason (R): Prime factorisation of 5 has only two factors, 1 and 5.
questions.answer_text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. A is true because 5ⁿ = (5)ⁿ contains no factor of 2, so it cannot end in 0. R is true (5 is prime) but does not directly explain A.

options.label: A
options.text: Both assertion (A) and reason (R) are true and reason (R) is the correct explanation of assertion (A)

options.label: B
options.text: Both assertion (A) and reason (R) are true and reason (R) is not the correct explanation of assertion (A)

options.label: C
options.text: Assertion (A) is true but reason (R) is false

options.label: D
options.text: Assertion (A) is false but reason (R) is true

Reason (R): HCF(65, 117) = 13
questions.answer_text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A. To find the number: HCF(70−5, 125−8) = HCF(65, 117) = 13.

questions.question_text: Explain why (7 × 13 × 11) + 11 and (7 × 6 × 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1) + 3 are composite numbers.
questions.answer_text: (7×13×11)+11 = 11(7×13+1) = 11×92. Since it has factors other than 1 and itself, it is composite. (7×6×5×4×3×2×1)+3 = 3(7×6×5×4×2×1+1) = 3×1681. Since it has factors other than 1 and itself, it is composite.

questions.question_text: Explain whether (3 × 12 × 101) + 4 is a prime number or a composite number.
questions.answer_text: (3×12×101)+4 = 4(3×3×101+1) = 4×910 = 2³×5×7×13. Since it has factors other than 1 and itself, it is a composite number.

questions.question_text: Given that HCF(306, 1314) = 18. Find LCM(306, 1314).
Check whether 4ⁿ can end with the digit 0 for any natural number n.
questions.answer_text: 4ⁿ = (2²)ⁿ = 2²ⁿ. The prime factorization of 4ⁿ contains only the prime 2, not 5. Since 10 = 2×5, for a number to end in 0 it must have both 2 and 5 as prime factors. Therefore 4ⁿ cannot end with the digit 0 for any natural number n.

questions.question_text: Show that 5√6 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume 5√6 is rational. Then 5√6 = p/q where p, q are integers, q ≠ 0. So √6 = p/(5q), which is rational. But √6 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore 5√6 is irrational.

questions.question_text: If two positive integers p and q are written as p = a²b³ and q = a³b where a and b are prime numbers then verify LCM(p, q) × HCF(p, q) = pq.
questions.answer_text: LCM(p,q) = a³b³, HCF(p,q) = a²b. LCM×HCF = a³b³×a²b = a⁵b⁴. pq = a²b³×a³b = a⁵b⁴. Since LCM×HCF = pq = a⁵b⁴, verified.

questions.question_text: Prove that 3 + √5 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume 3+√5 is rational = p/q. Then √5 = p/q − 3 = (p−3q)/q, which is rational. But √5 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore 3+√5 is irrational.

questions.question_text: Prove that 6 − √7 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume 6−√7 is rational = p/q. Then √7 = 6 − p/q = (6q−p)/q, which is rational. But √7 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore 6−√7 is irrational.

questions.question_text: Two numbers are in the ratio 2:3 and their LCM is 180. What is the HCF of these numbers?
questions.answer_text: Let the numbers be 2k and 3k. LCM(2k, 3k) = 6k = 180, so k = 30. HCF = k = 30.

questions.concept: HCF and LCM by prime factorization
questions.question_text: Using prime factorization find HCF and LCM of 96 and 120.
Find the greatest number which divides 85 and 72 leaving remainders 1 and 2 respectively.

questions.concept: Greatest number divisible by given numbers
questions.question_text: Find the greatest 3-digit number which is divisible by 18, 24 and 36.
questions.answer_text: LCM(18, 24, 36) = 72. Greatest 3-digit multiple of 72 = 72×13 = 936.

questions.question_text: Find the least number which when divided by 12, 16 and 24 leaves remainder 7 in each case.
questions.answer_text: LCM(12, 16, 24) = 48. Required number = 48 + 7 = 55.

questions.question_text: Find the smallest natural number by which 1200 should be multiplied so that the square root of the product is a rational number.
questions.answer_text: 1200 = 2⁴×3×5². For a perfect square, all prime exponents must be even. The exponent of 3 is 1 (odd), so multiply by 3. The product 1200×3 = 3600 = 60². Smallest natural number = 3.

questions.question_text: In a school, there are two sections of class X. There are 40 students in the first section and 48 students in the second section. Determine the minimum number of books required for their class library so that they can be distributed equally among students of both sections.
questions.answer_text: Minimum books = LCM(40, 48). 40 = 2³×5, 48 = 2⁴×3. LCM = 2⁴×3×5 = 240. Minimum 240 books required.

questions.question_text: Complete the following factor tree and find the composite number x. [Factor tree: x at top, factors 3 and 195; 195 splits into 3 and y; y splits into 5 and 13]
Complete the following factor tree and find the composite number x. [Factor tree: x at top, factors 2 and 3381; 3381 splits into 3 and ?; ? splits into 7 and 161; 161 splits into 7 and ?]
Prove that √5 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume √5 is rational. Then √5 = p/q where p, q are co-prime integers and q≠0. So 5 = p²/q², i.e. p² = 5q². Thus 5 divides p², so 5 divides p. Let p = 5m. Then 25m² = 5q², so q² = 5m². Thus 5 divides q², so 5 divides q. But then 5 is a common factor of p and q, contradicting that p and q are co-prime. Hence √5 is irrational.

questions.question_text: Given that √3 is an irrational, prove that 5 + 2√3 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume 5+2√3 is rational = p/q. Then 2√3 = p/q−5 = (p−5q)/q. So √3 = (p−5q)/(2q), which is rational. But √3 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore 5+2√3 is irrational.

questions.question_text: Given that √5 is an irrational, prove that 2√5 − 3 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume 2√5−3 is rational = p/q. Then 2√5 = p/q+3 = (p+3q)/q. So √5 = (p+3q)/(2q), which is rational. But √5 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore 2√5−3 is irrational.

questions.question_text: Given that √3 is an irrational, prove that (2−√3)/5 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume (2−√3)/5 is rational = p/q. Then 2−√3 = 5p/q. So √3 = 2−5p/q = (2q−5p)/q, which is rational. But √3 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore (2−√3)/5 is irrational.

questions.question_text: Given that √2 is an irrational, prove that 5 + 3√2 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: Assume 5+3√2 is rational = p/q. Then 3√2 = p/q−5 = (p−5q)/q. So √2 = (p−5q)/(3q), which is rational. But √2 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore 5+3√2 is irrational.

questions.question_text: Prove that (√2 + √3)² is irrational number, given that √6 is an irrational number.
questions.answer_text: (√2+√3)² = 2+2√6+3 = 5+2√6. Assume 5+2√6 is rational = p/q. Then 2√6 = p/q−5 = (p−5q)/q. So √6 = (p−5q)/(2q), which is rational. But √6 is irrational — contradiction. Therefore (√2+√3)² is irrational.

questions.question_text: Write the smallest number which is divisible by both 306 and 657.
questions.answer_text: Smallest number divisible by both = LCM(306, 657). 306 = 2×3²×17, 657 = 3²×73. LCM = 2×3²×17×73 = 22338.

questions.question_text: Find by prime factorization the LCM of the numbers 18180 and 7575. Also find the HCF of two numbers.

144 cartons of Coke cans and 90 cartons of Pepsi cans are to be stacked in a canteen. If each stack is of the same height and if it equal contain cartons of the same drink, what would be the greatest number of cartons each stack would have?
questions.answer_text: Greatest number of cartons per stack = HCF(144, 90). 144 = 2⁴×3², 90 = 2×3²×5. HCF = 2×3² = 18.

questions.question_text: Three bells toll at intervals of 9, 12, 15 minutes respectively. If they start tolling together, after what time will they next toll together?
questions.answer_text: LCM(9, 12, 15). 9 = 3², 12 = 2²×3, 15 = 3×5. LCM = 2²×3²×5 = 180 minutes = 3 hours. They will next toll together after 3 hours.

questions.question_text: Three bells ring at intervals of 6, 12 and 18 minutes. If all the three bells rang at 6 a.m., when will they ring together again?
questions.answer_text: LCM(6, 12, 18). 6 = 2×3, 12 = 2²×3, 18 = 2×3². LCM = 2²×3² = 36 minutes. They will ring together again at 6:36 a.m.

questions.question_text: Four bells toll at an interval of 8, 12, 15 and 18 seconds respectively. All the four begin to toll together. Find the number of times they toll together in one hour excluding the one at the start.

Three sets of English, Hindi and Mathematics books have to be stacked in such a way that all the books are stored topic wise and the height of each stack is the same. The number of English books is 96, the number of Hindi books is 240 and the number of Mathematics books is 336. Assuming that the books are of the same thickness.
questions.answer_text: HCF(96, 240, 336) = 48. Each stack has 48 books.

case_parts.part_text: Find the number of stacks of Hindi books?
case_parts.part_answer: Number of stacks of Hindi books = 240 ÷ 48 = 5.

case_parts.part_text: Find the number of stacks of English books? OR Find the number of stacks of Mathematics books?
What is the number of books that can be stored in each stack?

questions.question_text: Teaching Mathematics through activities is a powerful approach that enhances students’ understanding and engagement. Keeping this in mind, Ms. Muktha planned a prime number game for class 5 students. She announces the number 2in her class and asked the first student to multiply it by a prime number and then pass it to second student. Second student also multiplied it by a prime number and passed it to third student. In this way by multiplying to a prime number, the last student got 173250.
questions.answer_text: 173250 = 2 × 3² × 5³ × 7 × 11. Starting from 2, students multiplied by: 3, 3, 5, 5, 5, 7, 11 (7 multiplications = 7 students).

case_parts.part_text: What is the least prime number used by students?
case_parts.part_answer: 173250 = 2×3²×5³×7×11. Students multiplied using primes: 3, 3, 5, 5, 5, 7, 11. Least prime used by students = 3.

case_parts.part_text: How many students are in the class? OR What is the highest prime number used by students?
case_parts.part_answer: 173250 = 2×3²×5³×7×11. Total prime factors (with repetition) = 1+2+3+1+1 = 8. Starting number is 2 (1 factor), so students multiplied 7 times = 7 students. OR Highest prime used = 11.

case_parts.part_text: Which prime number has been used maximum times?
case_parts.part_answer: 173250 = 2×3²×5³×7×11. Among primes used by students (3,3,5,5,5,7,11), prime 5 appears 3 times. Prime used maximum times = 5.

Reason (R): HCF is always a factor of LCM.
questions.answer_text: A is true (LCM = 5780÷17 = 340). R is true (HCF is always a factor of LCM) but R is not the correct explanation of A (the correct explanation uses HCF×LCM = product). Answer: b).

questions.question_text: The length, breadth and height of a room are 8m 50cm, 6m 25cm and 4m 75cm respectively. Find the length of the longest rod that can measure the dimensions of the room exactly.

On a morning walk, three persons step off together and their steps measure 40 cm, 42 cm and 45 cm respectively. Find the minimum distance each should walk so that each can cover the same distance in complete steps.

The traffic lights at three different road crossings change after every 48 seconds, 72 seconds and 108 seconds respectively. If they change simultaneously at 7 a.m, at what time will they change together next?
questions.answer_text: LCM(48, 72, 108). 48=2⁴×3, 72=2³×3², 108=2²×3³. LCM=2⁴×3³=432 seconds = 7 min 12 sec. They will change together next at 7:07:12 a.m.

questions.question_text: National Art convention got registrations from students from all parts of the country, of which 60 are interested in music, 84 are interested in dance and 108 students are interested in handicrafts. For optimum cultural exchange, organisers wish to keep them in minimum number of groups such that each group consists of students interested in the same artform and the number of students in each group is the same. Find the number of students in each group. Find the number of groups in each art form. How many rooms are required if each group will be allotted a room?
questions.answer_text: Students per group = HCF(60, 84, 108). 60=2²×3×5, 84=2²×3×7, 108=2²×3³. HCF=2²×3=12. Groups: Music=60÷12=5, Dance=84÷12=7, Handicrafts=108÷12=9. Total rooms=5+7+9=21.

questions.question_text: In a teacher’s workshop the number of teacher teaching French, Hindi and English are 48, 80 and 144 respectively. Find the minimum number of rooms required if in each room the same numbers of teachers are seated and all of them are of same subject.

February 14 is celebrated as International Book Giving Day and many countries in the world celebrate this day. Some people in India also started celebrating this day and donated the following number of books of various subjects to a public library: History = 96, Science = 240, Mathematics = 336.

These books have to be arranged in minimum number of stacks such that each stack contains books of only one subject and the number of books on each stack is the same.

case_parts.part_text: How many books are arranged in each stack?
How many stacks are used to arrange all the Mathematics books?

(a) Determine the total number of stacks that will be used for arranging all the books.
OR
(b) If the thickness of each book of History, Science and Mathematics is 1.8 cm, 2.2 cm and 2.5 cm respectively, then find the height of each stack of History, Science and Mathematics books.
case_parts.part_answer:
(a) Total stacks = 96÷48 + 240÷48 + 336÷48 = 2+5+7 = 14 stacks.
OR
(b) History stack height = 48×1.8 = 86.4 cm. Science = 48×2.2 = 105.6 cm. Mathematics = 48×2.5 = 120 cm.
